The **Confederation of African Football**[[a]](#cite_note-2) (**CAF**) is the administrative and controlling body for [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [beach soccer](/wiki/Beach_soccer "Beach soccer"), and [futsal](/wiki/Futsal "Futsal") in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). It was officially established on 8 February 1957 at the Grand Hotel in [Khartoum](/wiki/Khartoum "Khartoum"), Sudan. The foundation followed a decision made at the 1954 [FIFA Congress](/wiki/FIFA_Congress "FIFA Congress") in [Bern](/wiki/Bern "Bern"), Switzerland, where Africa was formally recognised as a football confederation.[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Representing the African [confederation](/wiki/Geography_of_association_football "Geography of association football") of [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"), CAF organises runs and regulates [national team](/wiki/National_team "National team") and [club](/wiki/Football_team "Football team") [continental competitions](#CAF_competitions) annually or biennially such as the [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations") and [Women's Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Women%27s_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Women's Africa Cup of Nations"), which they control the prize money and [broadcast rights](/wiki/Broadcasting_of_sports_events "Broadcasting of sports events") to. CAF will be allocated 9 spots at the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") starting from [2026](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup") and could have an opportunity of 10 spots with the addition of an intercontinental play-off tournament involving 6 teams to decide the last 2 FIFA World Cup places (46+2).

The headquarters of CAF were originally located within the offices of the Sudanese Football Association in Khartoum, before being relocated to a site near [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), Egypt, following a fire. Youssef Mohamad served as the organisation’s first general secretary, and [Abdel Aziz Abdallah Salem](/wiki/Abdel_Aziz_Abdallah_Salem "Abdel Aziz Abdallah Salem") was its first [president](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_Confederation_of_African_Football "List of presidents of the Confederation of African Football"). The current president, [Patrice Motsepe](/wiki/Patrice_Motsepe "Patrice Motsepe") of [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), was first elected unopposed on 12 March 2021 in elections held in [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat "Rabat"), Morocco.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-CAFEC-7)

## Anthem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Anthem")]

CAF launched a competition for all African composers to create its [anthem](/wiki/Anthem "Anthem") without lyrics to reflect the cultural patrimony and the [music of Africa](/wiki/Music_of_Africa "Music of Africa") on 18 September 2007.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

## Members and zones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Members and zones")]

A total of 54 member associations are part of the Confederation of African Football.[[1]](#cite_note-Mem-1)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

[CECAFA](/wiki/CECAFA "CECAFA") (East Africa)

[COSAFA](/wiki/COSAFA "COSAFA") (Southern Africa)

Additionally, there are territories located in Africa which are not affiliated with CAF or any other confederation to any extent.

* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Flag_of_Mayotte_%28Local%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Mayotte_%28Local%29.svg.png) [Mayotte](/wiki/Ligue_mahoraise_de_football "Ligue mahoraise de football")
* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Flag_of_CSSC.svg/40px-Flag_of_CSSC.svg.png) [Chagos Islands](/wiki/Chagos_Football_Association "Chagos Football Association") (*[British Indian Ocean Territory](/wiki/British_Indian_Ocean_Territory "British Indian Ocean Territory")*)

Some African states with [limited or no international recognition](/wiki/List_of_states_with_limited_recognition "List of states with limited recognition") have official national teams, but none have been considered for CAF membership. Instead, they are affiliated with organisations such as [CONIFA](/wiki/Confederation_of_Independent_Football_Associations "Confederation of Independent Football Associations"). Somaliland is not a member of CONIFA after leaving in 2010s and is a candidate to join [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") after its recognition in the coming years.

* ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Flag_of_Somaliland.svg/40px-Flag_of_Somaliland.svg.png) [Somaliland](/wiki/Somaliland_Football_Association "Somaliland Football Association") FIFA code: **SMD**

### International

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: International")]

Shortly after formation, CAF organised the [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations") (abbreviated AFCON) in 1957 and it has since become its flagship competition. Faced with undisclosed decline in popularity of local competitions and the mass exodus of homegrown footballers to Europe, Asia and the Americas in the 1990s and early 2000s, CAF launched the [African Nations Championship](/wiki/African_Nations_Championship "African Nations Championship") (alternatively, though not widely used, the Championship of African Nations (CHAN)) on 11 September 2007 and began organisation two years later, to address this issue. CAF also organises qualification tournaments/competitions for the [FIFA U-20 World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_U-20_World_Cup "FIFA U-20 World Cup") and the [FIFA U-17 World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_U-17_World_Cup "FIFA U-17 World Cup") for its member associations; both of which initially began on a home-and-away [two-legged](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie") basis but has since 1995 been organised in appointed host countries as respectively the [Under-20](/wiki/U-20_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "U-20 Africa Cup of Nations") and [U-17 Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/U-17_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "U-17 Africa Cup of Nations").

For [women's football](/wiki/Women%27s_association_football "Women's association football") operates competitions which currently serve as qualification tournaments for the related FIFA-organised tournaments which launched at the exact same year they began formation. The flagship African women's football competition/tournament is the [Women's Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Women%27s_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Women's Africa Cup of Nations"), which launched in 1991 as the *African Women's Championship* and was known in the mass media between 2015 and 2021 as the *Africa/African Women/Women's Cup of Nations*, which currently qualifies 4 teams to the [FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA Women's World Cup"). CAF also organises qualification matches for "promising future female footballers" at both the [Under-20](/wiki/African_U-20_Women%27s_World_Cup_qualification "African U-20 Women's World Cup qualification") and [Under-17 levels](/wiki/African_U-17_Women%27s_World_Cup_qualification "African U-17 Women's World Cup qualification"), launched in 2002 and 2008 respectively, both of which crowns no champions but instead qualifies 2 teams to compete at the [FIFA U-20 Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_U-20_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA U-20 Women's World Cup") and the [FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_U-17_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup") respectively.

### Club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Club")]

For African clubs, CAF runs the [CAF Men's](/wiki/CAF_Champions_League "CAF Champions League") and [Women's Champions League](/wiki/CAF_Women%27s_Champions_League "CAF Women's Champions League"), the [CAF Confederation Cup](/wiki/CAF_Confederation_Cup "CAF Confederation Cup"), the [CAF Super Cup](/wiki/CAF_Super_Cup "CAF Super Cup") and the African Schools Football Championship for both males and females. First held in 1964 as the *African Cup of Champions Clubs* (simply known sometimes as the **African Cup**) and rebranded in 1997 as the CAF Champions League, this football club competition currently features the champions of top-division leagues of CAF member associations and the runners-up teams of the league classifications of member associations the top 12 ranked national associations as documented by the [CAF 5-year ranking](/wiki/CAF_5-year_ranking "CAF 5-year ranking") system.

A currently-former competition, the [African Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/African_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "African Cup Winners' Cup"), commenced in 1975 for national cup winners of member associations and a third currently-former competition, the [CAF Cup](/wiki/CAF_Cup "CAF Cup"), launched in 1992 for African teams who finished below the top 2 positions of the league classifications of member associations and haven't met any criteria for qualification to any CAF competition. CAF decided to merge these two competitions together to form the current second-tier CAF Confederation Cup in 2004, and it currently incorporates the participation of national cup winners from the Cup Winners' Cup, whiles maintaining the format of the participation of teams who finished 3rd in the top-division league classifications of the 12 highest-ranked member associations as documented by the CAF 5-Year Ranking system from the CAF Cup. It is also ranked below the CAF Champions League.[[12]](#cite_note-16)

The winners of the CAF Champions League play the winners of the African Cup Winners' Cup until 2004 and the CAF Confederation Cup thereafter in the [CAF Super Cup](/wiki/CAF_Super_Cup "CAF Super Cup") which was launched in 1993.

The [Afro-Asian Club Championship](/wiki/Afro-Asian_Club_Championship "Afro-Asian Club Championship") was an annual football match jointly organised between CAF and the [Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") (AFC) between the winners of the CAF Champions League and the winners of the [AFC Champions League](/wiki/AFC_Champions_League "AFC Champions League") between 1987 and 1999.

The CAF Women's Champions League was announced and approved on 30 June 2020, launched on 12 September that year and began contesting the following year, i.e. 2021.[[13]](#cite_note-17)[[14]](#cite_note-18) It features women's national league and cup winners nvolving the champions of CAF's sub-confederation qualification tournaments for women's club teams.

## Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

In October 2004, South African telecommunications giant, [MTN](/wiki/MTN_Group "MTN Group"), contracted a 4-year deal to sponsor CAF competitions worth US$12.5 million, which was the biggest sponsorship deal in African sporting history at that time.[[15]](#cite_note-21)

CAF opened new sponsorship callouts when MTN's contract expired and French telecommunications giant [Orange](/wiki/Orange_S.A. "Orange S.A.") scooped it up in July 2009, signing an 8-year comprehensive long-term undisclosed deal to sponsor CAF competitions with a value of €100 million.[[16]](#cite_note-22)

On 21 July 2016, French energy and petroleum giant, Total S.A., replaced Orange as the main sponsor with an 8-year sponsorship package from CAF for a value of €950 million[[17]](#cite_note-23) to support its competitions.[[18]](#cite_note-24) Total rebranded as [TotalEnergies](/wiki/TotalEnergies "TotalEnergies") on 28 May 2021.[[19]](#cite_note-25)

The current main CAF sponsors are:

* [1xBet](/wiki/1xBet "1xBet")
* [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok")
* [TotalEnergies](/wiki/TotalEnergies "TotalEnergies")
* [Orange](/wiki/Orange_S.A. "Orange S.A.")

### Non-CAF competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Non-CAF competition")]

The 1982 African Super Cup is a match which took place on 25 January 1982 during the [Tournament of Fraternity](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tournoi_de_la_Fraternit%C3%A9_1982 "fr:Tournoi de la Fraternité 1982") in [Abidjan](/wiki/Abidjan "Abidjan"), Ivory Coast. The [Kabyles](/wiki/Kabyle_people "Kabyle people") of [JS Kabylie](/wiki/JS_Kabylie "JS Kabylie") won this trophy against the [Cameroonians](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") of [Union Douala](/wiki/Union_Douala "Union Douala").[[22]](#cite_note-28)

### By country

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: By country")]

The following table lists all the countries whose clubs have won at least one CAF competition. Egyptian clubs are the most successful, with a total of 44 titles. Egyptian clubs hold a record number of wins in [the African Cup of Champions Clubs/CAF Champions League](/wiki/CAF_Champions_League "CAF Champions League") (19), the now-defunct [African Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/African_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "African Cup Winners' Cup") (8), the [CAF Super Cup](/wiki/CAF_Super_Cup "CAF Super Cup") (12) and the now-defunct [Afro-Asian Club Championship](/wiki/Afro-Asian_Club_Championship "Afro-Asian Club Championship") (3), followed by Tunisian clubs with 24 titles and they have the most victories in the now-defunct [CAF Cup](/wiki/CAF_Cup "CAF Cup") (4) and Moroccan clubs have secured also 24 titles with the most victories in the [CAF Confederation Cup](/wiki/CAF_Confederation_Cup "CAF Confederation Cup") (7).

## Major tournament records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Major tournament records")]

*Legend*

* 1st – Champions
* 2nd – Runners-up
* 3rd – Third place
* 4th – Fourth place
* QF – Quarter-finals (1934–1938, 1954–1970, and 1986–present: knockout round of 8)
* R3 — Round 3 (2026–present: knockout round of 16)
* R2 — Round 2 (1974–1978: second group stage, top 8; 1982: second group stage, top 12; 1986–2022: knockout round of 16; 2026–present: knockout round of 32)
* R1 — Round 1 (1930, 1950–1970 and 1986–present: group stage; 1934–1938: knockout round of 16; 1974–1982: first group stage)
* *Q* — Qualified for upcoming tournament
* ••  – Qualified but withdrew
* •  – Did not qualify
* ×  – Did not enter / Withdrew / Banned
* – Hosts
* – Not affiliated in FIFA

For each tournament, the flag of the host country and the number of teams in each finals tournament (in brackets) are shown.

*Firsts*

* 1934: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Flag_of_Egypt_%281922-1958%29.svg/40px-Flag_of_Egypt_%281922-1958%29.svg.png) [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_national_football_team "Egypt national football team") first African team to qualify for the World Cup
* 1970: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Flag_of_Morocco.svg/40px-Flag_of_Morocco.svg.png) [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco_national_football_team "Morocco national football team") first African team to draw a match in the World Cup
* 1978: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Flag_of_Tunisia.svg/40px-Flag_of_Tunisia.svg.png) [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia_national_football_team "Tunisia national football team") first African team to win a match in the World Cup
* 1982: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Flag_of_Algeria.svg/40px-Flag_of_Algeria.svg.png) [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria_national_football_team "Algeria national football team") first African team to win two matches in the World Cup
* 1986: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Flag_of_Algeria.svg/40px-Flag_of_Algeria.svg.png) [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria_national_football_team "Algeria national football team") first African team to qualify for two consecutive World Cups
* 1986: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Flag_of_Morocco.svg/40px-Flag_of_Morocco.svg.png) [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco_national_football_team "Morocco national football team") first African team to reach the knockout stage (round of sixteen)
* 1990: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Flag_of_Cameroon.svg/40px-Flag_of_Cameroon.svg.png) [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team") first African team to reach the knockout stage (quarter-finals)
* 1994 and 1998: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Flag_of_Nigeria.svg/40px-Flag_of_Nigeria.svg.png) [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria_national_football_team "Nigeria national football team") first African team to top a group stage and reach the knockout stage (round of 16) in two consecutive World Cups
* 2002: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Flag_of_Senegal.svg/40px-Flag_of_Senegal.svg.png) [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal_national_football_team "Senegal national football team") first African team to reach the knockout stage (quarter-finals) on their World Cup debut
* 2010: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Flag_of_South_Africa.svg/40px-Flag_of_South_Africa.svg.png) [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_soccer_team "South Africa national soccer team") first African team to host the World Cup
* 2014: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Flag_of_Algeria.svg/40px-Flag_of_Algeria.svg.png) [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria_national_football_team "Algeria national football team") & ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Flag_of_Nigeria.svg/40px-Flag_of_Nigeria.svg.png) [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria_national_football_team "Nigeria national football team") first African teams to reach the knockout stage (round of sixteen) simultaneously in the World Cup
* 2022: ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Flag_of_Morocco.svg/40px-Flag_of_Morocco.svg.png) [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco_national_football_team "Morocco national football team") first African team to reach the knockout stage (semi-finals), taking fourth place

## CAF Best Footballers of the Century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: CAF Best Footballers of the Century")]

## CAF Golden Jubilee Best Players poll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: CAF Golden Jubilee Best Players poll")]

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Confederation_of_African_Football&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Confédération Africaine de Football*; [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الاتحاد الإفريقي لكرة القدم, [romanised](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *al-Ittiḥād al-Ifrīqī li-Kurat al-Qadam*; [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Confederação Africana de Futebol*; [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Confederación Africana de Fútbol*; [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"): *Shirikisho la Soka Afrika*.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** Member of [UNAF](/wiki/UNAF "UNAF"). Withdrew on 19 November 2009 and rejoined in 2011.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** Excluded from CAF and from the [1957 African Cup of Nations](/wiki/1957_African_Cup_of_Nations "1957 African Cup of Nations") due to [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid").
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-AM_14-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-AM_14-1) Associate member, not part of [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"). Zanzibar held full membership for four months in 2017, when its status was changed after CAF admitted its membership was an error.[[11]](#cite_note-15) However, Zanzibar is a full-member of [CECAFA](/wiki/CECAFA "CECAFA").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** This tournament was initially formed as a home-and-away qualification tournament for U-21 African nations in 1977. Since 1979, a proper tournament was launched as the *African Youth Championship* and used these branded titles until 2015: *African U-21 Cup of Nations* until 1989, *African U-21 Championship* until 2003 and *African U-20 Championship* until 2015. The current name was adopted in 2017.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** From 1995 to 2015, the tournament was known as the *African U-17 Championship*. The current name was adopted in 2017.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-30)** [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") gained independence in 1962, but they joined with other African nations to boycott the [1966 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"). Thus the [1970 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1970 FIFA World Cup qualification") was their first participation.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola") gained independence in 1975. Thus the [1986 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1986 FIFA World Cup qualification") was their first participation.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-Zaire_32-0)** The [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") competed as [Zaire](/wiki/Zaire "Zaire") in 1974.
10. **[^](#cite_ref-33)** [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") gained independence in 1960, but they joined with other African nations to boycott the [1966 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"). Thus the [1974 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1974 FIFA World Cup qualification") was their first participation.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-34)** Egypt team represented the [United Arab Republic](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic") with Syria in 1960 finishing the 12th and alone in 1964 finishing the 4th.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-35)** Costa Rica and Panama were originally due to host the [2020 FIFA U-20 Women's World Cup](/wiki/2020_FIFA_U-20_Women%27s_World_Cup "2020 FIFA U-20 Women's World Cup"), but the latter withdrew citing [the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Panama "COVID-19 pandemic in Panama") and left Costa Rica as the sole hosts. FIFA postponed the 2020 edition to the following year, i.e. 2021, pending improvement in pandemic management, but cancelled it altogether on 17 November 2020 due to the escalation of the pandemic caused by the discovery of [the COVID-19 Omicron variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Omicron_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant") a month earlier and automatically awarded them the 2022 edition.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-37)** Original hosts Peru were stripped of the rights to host the 2019 edition in February that year.[[24]](#cite_note-36)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-38)** Peru was originally due to host the [2021 FIFA U-17 World Cup](/wiki/2021_FIFA_U-17_World_Cup "2021 FIFA U-17 World Cup") but FIFA cancelled it on 24 December 2020 citing the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") and its escalation of the pandemic caused by the discovery of [the COVID-19 Omicron variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Omicron_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant") a month earlier as the reasons and automatically awarded them the 2023 edition. Peru later withrew as hosts on 2 May 2023 due to infrastructural defects and FIFA awarded the hosting eights to Indonesia, whom FIFA earlier stripped the hosting rights for the year's FIFA U-20 World Cup.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** India were originally due to host the [2020 FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup](/wiki/2021_FIFA_U-17_Women%27s_World_Cup "2021 FIFA U-17 Women's World Cup"), but it was postponed to the following year, i.e. 2021, due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") by FIFA, who eventually cancelled it on 17 November 2020 and rather automatically awarded them the 2022 edition.
